title: Internal Laminar Flow
text:
Couette Flow
Consider two parallel plates with infinite span separated by a constant density fluid of height . Each plate is a distance of  away from the centerline. Neglect buoyancy effects and assume the top plate is moving at a constant velocity of  while the bottom plate is stationary. Furthermore, assume the bottom and top plates are isothermal at temperatures of  and  respectively. The flow considered here is called parallel flow as all streamlines are parallel. Since all fluid elements move parallel to each other,  and constant density mass conservation is

Therefore, the velocity profile  can only be a function of . Assuming steady flow of a Newtonian fluid, the momentum equation in the  and  directions simplify to

Momentum in the  direction shows there is no pressure gradient in the  direction. There also cannot be a pressure gradient in the  direction as that would violate continuity. If we assumed , then this would require  so , which violates continuity. The PDE above reduces down to

subject to the boundary conditions  and . Integrating twice to determine the velocity profile gives

The energy equation for a constant density fluid is

title: Chapter 4. Application of Numerical Methods to Model Equations
text:
The Burgers’ equation serves as an analog to the Navier Stokes equations. The equation includes an unsteady term, a nonlinear convection term, and a viscous term.

When the viscous term is included, equation (1) is parabolic. The inviscid Burgers’ equation arises when viscous effects are zero, .

Equation (2) may be viewed as a nonlinear wave equation where each point on the wave front can propagate at different speeds. The linear, 1D convection equation considered earlier is a simpler case of this, where the propagation speed of all waves was at a constant speed. Since Burgers’ equation contains varying wave speeds, shocks may be modeled due to the coalescing of wave information.
Nonlinear hyperbolic PDEs exhibit two types of solutions according to Lax (1954). Consider the general case of the equation below. Introduce the Jacobian  from the chain rule.



First order methods are rarely used in solving hyperbolic equations owing to their dissipative nature. The Lax method is typically used to demonstrate its dissipative nature and the application of a first order method to a nonlinear equation.

The first term is  averaged between its neighboring cells. This introduces artificial viscosity into the method that smooths out any instabilities or oscillations in the solution.






NoteDerivation of the Lax Method





Expand  about  using a Taylor series and substitute the time derivative term with the spatial derivative. From equation (3), we have that . Therefore

Neglecting the higher order terms and applying a central difference scheme on , the Lax method is obtained. An average is used for calculating the  term to introduce numerical dissipation.




The amplification factor for this scheme is given below, where  and . Since  for Burgers’ equation, .

Stability occurs when  and equation (5) reduces down to

where  is the maximum eigenvalue of the Jacobian .



The Lax-Wendroff method was one of the first second order finite difference methods derived for hyperbolic PDEs. Once again, we consider the general form of the governing equation (3). The Lax-Wendroff method is

There are many different ways of evaluating the Jacobian matrix at the half interval . For Lax-Wendroff, one way is to use a simple average of the neighboring cells. Since , then we have

For Burgers’ equation,  so the half interval Jacobians reduce down to







NoteDerivation of the Lax-Wendroff Method





Start off in the same manner as the Lax method and expand  about  using a Taylor series.

Next, replace the time derivatives with spatial derivatives. From equation (3), the first time derivative is

For the second derivative term, differentiate the equation above with respect to time and interchange the differentiation order.

The last step is due to the chain rule. Substitute into the Taylor series to obtain

Apply central differencing schemes to all derivative terms. The second order derivative is

The  terms when evaluated at the cell interface utilize a central difference, as  and  are both exactly  away from the boundary. Therefore, central differences are used to decompose all derivative terms into their stencil approximations and the second order accuracy is maintained. Putting everything together gives the Lax-Wendroff method.



The amplification factor is

When , the scheme is stable. For the amplification above, this simplifies down to

When Lax-Wendroff is applied to the example problem, the method correctly predicts the position of the discontinuity. Moreover, the solution is relatively sharp and well-defined, with some oscillations occurring at either ends of the discontinuity. In comparison, the Lax method was purely monotone and did not exhibit any oscillatory motion. As the Courant number is decreased, the number of oscillations in the Lax-Wendroff solution increases as the solution quality is degraded.

title: Chapter 7. Turbulent and Transitional Internal Flow
text:
Introduction
In external flows, freestream conditions are typically unaffected by the boundaries. In contrast, due to the confined nature of internal flows, the wall presence has significant impact on the mean flow behavior. If fully developed, turbulent, internal flow is occuring in a smooth pipe, the flow field may be divided into three sublayers. The viscous sublayer is closest to the wall and extends to . The buffer zone extends beyond to , and the outer layer occupies the rest of the flow field. The outer outer layer is dominated by turbulent eddies whereas very near the wall, the effect of wall curvature is small, so the fluid is “unaware” that the flow field is confined on all sides. Therefore, the three correlations for u+ all apply.
crumbs: Chapter 7. Turbulent and Transitional Internal Flow
